content:
Sharing details of a new course, "Technology, Disability and Techno-ableism," offered by the Centre for Disability Studies (CDS), NALSAR, in collaboration with NPTEL.

The course examines whether technology genuinely advances inclusion or merely reinforces existing patterns of exclusion. It covers the medical, social, and human rights models of disability, the values embedded in technological design, and concepts such as universal design, reasonable accommodation, and co-design. It also foregrounds experiential expertise, treating disabled people not merely as users of technology but as knowers whose lived experience ought to inform design from the outset.

The course is led by Dr Amita Dhanda, Professor Emerita and Head of CDS, and Nilesh Singit, Distinguished Research Fellow and Afrah Asif with support from several practitioners and researchers working across disability rights and accessibility.

Who Should Join: Students, educators, designers, developers, researchers, policymakers, social workers, persons with disabilities, and anyone who works with or alongside the disability sector.

The course is free and hosted on the Swayam Learning Platform

Course Details: [Date, Duration etc..] https://lnkd.in/dqg3c5GZ
Course Certificate Details: https://lnkd.in/d9Re7XT6


Enrolments are open. You may register here: https://lnkd.in/dqqbCTCh

I should be glad if members could share this within their own networks as well.

I should be glad if members could share this within their own networks as well.

comment: One implementation challenge is that accessibility often gets treated as a compliance checkpoint. Bringing lived experience into procurement and product decisions is where design choices usually change.
